reviews.review_text: I can finally stop larping, now time to make edits of jacket with payday 2 voicelines

I put off playing Hotline Miami for far too long. Despite knowing of its impact and legacy in the indie scene and being a fan of top-down action, I always passed up the game in my mind as being just a gore fest. While it's a very violent game, it also serves as a deconstruction of violence in video games as a whole, which ended up really earning my respect.

[i]Do you like hurting other people? Who are leaving messages on your answering machine? Where are you right now? And the final question: Why are we having this conversation?[/i]

These are questions posed early on in the game and it only outright gives one answer through dialogue by the end of the game. This leaves a lot of room for player interpretation, there's a real question of [i]why[/i] the player and the character are both so willing to commit so much violence for seemingly mundane reasons. It's fantastic storytelling done through gameplay as much as it's done through dialogue.

The gameplay itself does stand out as well, the graphics are constantly disorienting with wavy camera movement and neon colors leads to intense yet surreal action moments. You usually die in one shot and so do enemies, so every level has a puzzle element as it's never as simple as charging through the whole level, especially when different enemies must be killed in different ways.

Easily one of the highlights of the game was the decision to use many different artists being used for individual missions. It gives each level a unique feel while still being cohesive in identity, and is a great way to be exposed to new artists.

Hotline Miami is a very important piece of art that also survives the test of time, as its very blunt and bleak view on violence in the world only becomes more relevant as time goes on, this game and its sequel are must plays.

I’m a bit late to the party, but wow, what a game. I absolutely love the design, the music, the chaos, and the subtle storytelling. To be honest, I am incredibly bad at it, but it’s still an absolute blast to play. Highly recommended!

Hotline Miami [7.8/10]

Story [7/10]: Hotline Miami's story is surprisingly simple on a superficial level. However, the developers included an appreciable meta-narrative subtext long before such approaches became common. However if story is what you're primarily looking for in a game right now, this may not be the right time to play Hotline Miami.

Gameplay [9/10]: The gameplay is extremely fun. It can be described as a murderous puzzle game where speed, technique, and precision are rewarded. Using only a few simple controls, the game pushes the player toward a level of efficiency and fluidity that very few titles manage to achieve. The gameplay loop is enriched by tactical decisions both before and during each encounter, all aimed at maximizing execution efficiency. Every move matters, every step could be your last, and this series of deadly puzzles is remarkably well designed. Without such refined game design, this game would be worth only half of what it ultimately delivers.

Length [10/10]: The game's completion time can range anywhere from twenty minutes to ten hours. This is not a joke. Since it is essentially a puzzle-action game, a player who knows every timing and has mastered the mechanics can finish it in around twenty minutes. That's extraordinary and speaks volumes about the developers' excellent game design. First-time players, on the other hand, should expect a seemingly endless cycle of trial and error. Be patient; by the end of the game, you'll feel genuinely satisfied with yourself.

Side Content [6/10]: The game does not feature side quests in the traditional sense, but it allows players to unlock increasingly useful and tactical items. This creates a wide range of possible combinations between masks, weapons, and overall strategies. Unlocking these items requires skill, as they are earned through points gained by executing levels with precision. The more you play, the more items you unlock, and the more you want to experiment with new combinations. However, it's clear that repeating the same gameplay loop in order to unlock items for that very same gameplay loop may not appeal to everyone, myself included. For that reason, this category only earns a passing grade.

Difficulty [10/10]: Hotline Miami has an incredible difficulty range. Everything kills you in a single hit, and you kill almost everything in a single hit as well (bosses excluded). This condition is established from the very first level and constantly keeps you under pressure, because even the last remaining enemy can be fatal if not approached carefully. The combination of demanding gameplay, outstanding level design, and the constant threat of instant death creates one of the most intense gaming experiences I have ever personally had.

Note: The difficulty increases exponentially as you progress through the game.

Replayability [6/10]: Replayability is closely tied to the side-content discussion. Everything depends on how willing the player is to improve their level of perfection after already beating the game. If you enjoy mastering mechanics and refining your performance, you'll probably give this category a higher score. If, on the other hand, you dislike replaying the same content repeatedly—as I do—you'll likely ignore or even dislike this aspect of the gameplay.

SFX [7/10]: The game is virtually bug-free, features excellent sound mixing, and makes great use of its soundtrack. The only real flaw is the visual presentation—not because pixel art is a problem, but because in a game that demands extreme precision, floor textures can sometimes obscure weapon locations, making them difficult to see. This happened to me several times and often resulted in death.

Endgame [7/10]: Hotline Miami's ending is exactly what you would expect after spending several hours with the game and understanding its narrative progression. More disappointing is the epilogue, which attempts to introduce a meta-narrative angle without delivering a truly meaningful twist. Personally, I would have preferred the game to end with the pre-epilogue finale, which already serves as a satisfying conclusion to a brief but bloody adventure.

Additional Notes: If you're a fan of Kill Bill, you'll love this game.

Hotline Miami:

Played with controller, you should probably not. 

Hotline Miami is a mysterious game about a henchman being called to kill without any questions. During your whole playthrough, you will ask yourself what this game is all about. So, story-based, it is great. The gameplay is addictive too. The game pushes you to try to use as many weapons as you can and each weapon can be used in many ways. There are also masks that will help you in your run. You will have to be very strategic in order to go through the levels, it may not seem to be the case, but the game is brutally hard and unforgiving: you die in almost any cases in one hit. 

However, Hotline Miami has some flaws that are very annoying and totally breaks the needed tight reflexes and precision:

- too much RNG dependant. Enemies don't spawn at the exact same place in the room and sometimes open doors that they usually don't, making it hard to find a pattern in a room and go for a consistent path. 
- there is no "drop the weapon without picking another" button (playing bare fists brings some advantages). It will always swap it to another weapon, if there is one nearby, which is a problem:  when you kill foes, they drop their weapon and dropping yours will in fact make it swap your weapon against the one of the defeated foe, which breaks your strategy. 
- clunky controls, I understand why it is needed to separate the character movement direction and orientation but tbh it is more often restrictive to have to use left AND right stick at the same time than to restrain only the left stick to be used so that it controls both the orientation and direction of the character at the same time. This is done so because it is sometimes needed to move in a direction and shoot in another, but there should have been an option to allow movement direction with left stick and orientation with right OR movement and orientation with left stick so that we can adapt during situations
- Locking enemies is not well made. You have to use the right stick to angle your character so that it is focused on a specific foe, than press the lock button. BUT, sometimes, the lock on decides to focus itself on the nearest enemy, not the one you focused, even if it is 90 degrees away from the enemy you wanted to focus. Needless to say that if this happened, you will angle in the wrong direction and will focus someone you don't want to, often resulting in a death.

It took me 25.5 hours to 100% the game, and between 15 and 20 hours to finish the base game (I'm bad at it, you might go faster). Regarding the achievements, they are grindy, so you might want to pass them if you are not into it. I might buy Hotline miami 2 just because of the story, that can be used to explain the first game.

amazing game 10/10 sound track gameplay everything even got my bf into it and hes not really a combat guy and got alot of my friend into it too they loved watching me stream it for them thanks for making such a amazing game

I absolutely loved the first Hotline Miami and even went as far as 100% completing it. The fast-paced gameplay is still some of the most satisfying I've experienced in any top-down shooter. Every level feels like a deadly puzzle where a single mistake can end your run, but pulling off a perfect clear is incredibly rewarding.

At this point, I've pretty much given up hope for a Hotline Miami 3. It's been years since Hotline Miami 2 released, and while I'd love to see the franchise return, it doesn't seem likely anytime soon. That said, the original game still holds up remarkably well today.

Performance is excellent, which comes as no surprise. The game runs flawlessly and can be played on practically any modern system without issues.

The story is surprisingly deep and mysterious, encouraging you to piece together what's really happening as you progress. It manages to be both disturbing and fascinating at the same time, making it far more memorable than most games in the genre.

And then there's the soundtrack. Simply put, it's an absolute banger. Every track perfectly complements the intense gameplay, and many of them are still stuck in my head years later. Few games have music that fits their atmosphere this well.

Overall, Hotline Miami is an amazing fast-paced shooter with addictive gameplay, an unforgettable story, and one of the best video game soundtracks ever made. Easy 10/10 from me, and a game I'd recommend to almost anyone.

[h1] Violence Has Better Music Than Memory [/h1]

I was addicted to Hotline Miami back in college. It sat in the same corner of my brain as Audiosurf: weird indie games that felt completely different from everything else at the time. After finishing a couple of very long JRPGs, I finally came back to it and realized something.

The game still holds up.

Under all the neon and hallucinations, it's a brutally simple machine. Every level is basically a puzzle disguised as a massacre. Once you learn enemy placements and figure out when to lure people in or charge straight at them, the game becomes less about surviving and more about execution. The violence turns into muscle memory.

Getting every mask can feel a bit like a chore, but that's also where a lot of the replay value comes from. Each new ability or playstyle changes how you approach familiar levels, and for such a simple game, it stays surprisingly fresh for a long time.

And then there's the soundtrack. Absolute banger. Several tracks hit me with memories I didn't even know I still had stored away. For anyone revisiting the game, it's a serious nostalgia rush. For newcomers, it's still one of the best soundtracks ever attached to a digital crime scene.

Games age. Trends fade. Hotline Miami is still a blood-soaked fever dream with incredible music and a style that hasn't lost its edge. There's a reason people still talk about it. There's a reason someone like Suda51 became a fan and even referenced it in Travis Strikes Again: No More Heroes.

One of the best games of all time, Every puzzle makes you think and approach it differently from the last while rewarding different styles of gameplay for each one. The story and visuals all compliment each other along with the most banger soundtrack in retro gaming. It truly embodies the style of retro Miami and makes you enjoy killing other people.

About 5.3 hours of gameplay counting my mess ups. It was such a blast that I even went back to perfect some of the levels I struggled on. Honestly, for 10 dollars this game is great if you had a long day and wanna binge a story game with fun movement and gameplay. That's about it.

Hotline Miami is honestly one of the most unique games I’ve played. Even though it came out years ago, the gameplay feels really smooth and fast. The combat is super satisfying once you get the hang of it, and the soundtrack makes every level feel fun.
The game isn’t very long, but I believe in my opinion that actually works in its favor since it never really gets boring. You can finish the main story pretty quickly, but if your a player who keeps going for achievements and better scores I would say it gives you a better reason to keep playing.
If you like fast paced games with great music and don’t mind dying a lot while learning, I’d definitely Strongly recommend it.

Unplayable on pc with the camera controls. The mouse moves dependently of the character. A truly mind-boggling game design choice... Never in my life have I seen a top down game where the cursor will move with your character. 
The result of this is that it's basically impossible (or at least insanely difficult) to do something simple like dodge an enemy and fight back at the same time. You literally have to multitask counteracting your movements with the mouse when avoiding enemy attacks, and trying to land a shot at the same time. The best part is there is no way to change the camera controls. It's not like that on console, and no other top down game does this. seriouly wtf... Just play intravenous or something, this game is not the move.
